❤️ Heart Disease Prediction using Machine Learning¶

Problem Statement¶

The objective of this project is to build a Machine Learning model that predicts whether a patient is likely to have heart disease based on clinical and medical attributes.

Project Workflow¶

Project Workflow¶

  1. Import Libraries

  2. Load Dataset

  3. Dataset Overview

  4. Data Cleaning

  5. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

  6. Feature Engineering

  7. Define Features and Target Variable

  8. Train-Test Split

  9. Feature Scaling

  10. Model Building

  • Logistic Regression
  • Decision Tree
  • Random Forest
  1. Model Evaluation
  • Logistic Regression Evaluation
  • Decision Tree Evaluation
  • Random Forest Evaluation
  1. Hyperparameter Tuning
  2. Feature Importance
  3. Cross Validation
  4. Model Comparison
  5. Save and Load Best Model
  6. New Patient Prediction
  7. Conclusion

1. Import Required Libraries¶

In [1]:
# Data Analysis
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
# Data Visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
# Data Preprocessing
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split,GridSearchCV,cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
# Machine Learning Models
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
# Model Evaluation
from sklearn.metrics import (accuracy_score,confusion_matrix,classification_report,roc_curve,roc_auc_score)
# Model Saving
import joblib

2. Load Dataset¶

In [2]:
df=pd.read_csv("Heart disease dataset.csv")

4. Data Cleaning¶

In [8]:
df.isnull().sum()
Out[8]:
age         0
sex         0
cp          0
trestbps    0
chol        0
fbs         0
restecg     0
thalach     0
exang       0
oldpeak     0
slope       0
ca          4
thal        2
num         0
dtype: int64
In [9]:
df["ca"]=df["ca"].fillna(df["ca"].median())
df["thal"]=df["thal"].fillna(df["thal"].median())
In [10]:
df.isnull().sum()

ca          0
thal        0

df.duplicated().sum()

np.int64(0)

5. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

5.1 Target Variable Distribution¶

In [12]:
#Convert Target Variable into Binary Classes
df["num"]=df["num"].apply(lambda x: 0 if x==0 else 1)
In [13]:
df["num"].value_counts()
Out[13]:
num
0    164
1    139
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [14]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x="num",data=df)
plt.title("Target Variable Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Heart Disease Class")
plt.ylabel("Number of Patients")
plt.savefig("target_variable_distribution.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

5.2 Age Distribution¶

In [15]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.histplot(df["age"],bins=15,kde=True)

plt.ylabel("Frequency")

plt.show()

5.10 Relationship Analysis¶

Gender vs Heart Disease¶

In [29]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x="sex",hue="num",data=df)
plt.title("Gender vs Heart Disease")
plt.xlabel("Gender (0=Female,1=Male)")
plt.ylabel("Number of Patients")
plt.legend(title="Heart Disease")
plt.show()

Chest Pain Type vs Heart Disease¶

In [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x="cp",hue="num",data=df)
plt.title("Chest Pain Type vs Heart Disease")
plt.xlabel("Chest Pain Type")
plt.ylabel("Number of Patients")
plt.legend(title="Heart Disease")
plt.show()

Age vs Heart Disease¶

In [31]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.boxplot(x="num",y="age",data=df)
plt.title("Age vs Heart Disease")
plt.xlabel("Heart Disease (0 = No, 1 = Yes)")
plt.ylabel("Age")
plt.show()

EDA Summary¶

The exploratory data analysis provided important insights into the dataset. Most patients belonged to the middle-aged group, and male patients were more common than female patients. The target variable showed a reasonably balanced distribution. Correlation analysis indicated that some features had stronger relationships with heart disease, while many features were only weakly correlated with each other. These observations helped in understanding the dataset before building machine learning models.

6. Feature Engineering¶

The dataset is already well-structured and does not require additional feature engineering. Existing features are directly used for model building.

7. Define Features and Target Variable¶

In [32]:
X=df.drop("num",axis=1)
y=df["num"]

8. Train-Test Split¶

The dataset is divided into training and testing sets. The training set is used to train the machine learning models, while the testing set is used to evaluate their performance on unseen data.

In [33]:
X_train,X_test,y_train,y_test=train_test_split(X,y,test_size=0.20,random_state=42)
In [34]:
print("Training Set:",X_train.shape)
print("Testing Set :",X_test.shape)

Training Set: (242, 13)
Testing Set : (61, 13)

9. Feature Scaling¶

Feature scaling standardizes the numerical features so that they have a similar scale. This helps distance-based and gradient-based machine learning algorithms perform better.

In [35]:
scaler=StandardScaler()
In [36]:
X_train=scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
In [37]:
X_test=scaler.transform(X_test)

10. Model Building¶

10.1 Logistic Regression¶

Logistic Regression is used as the baseline classification model because the target variable contains two classes (Heart Disease: Yes or No). It provides a simple and interpretable benchmark for comparing the performance of other machine learning models.

In [38]:
lr_model=LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000,random_state=42)

Train the Model¶

In [39]:
lr_model.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[39]:
LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)

Make Predictions¶

In [40]:
lr_pred=lr_model.predict(X_test)

Training and Testing Accuracy¶

In [41]:
print("Training Accuracy :",round(lr_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),"%")
print("Testing Accuracy :",round(lr_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),"%")

Training Accuracy : 84.3 %
Testing Accuracy : 88.52 %

10.2 Decision Tree¶

Decision Tree is trained to capture non-linear relationships between the input features and the target variable. It helps compare the performance of a tree-based model with Logistic Regression.

In [42]:
dt_model=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)

Train the Model¶

In [43]:
dt_model.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[43]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)

Make Predictions¶

In [44]:
dt_pred=dt_model.predict(X_test)

Training and Testing Accuracy¶

In [45]:
print("Training Accuracy :",round(dt_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),"%")
print("Testing Accuracy :",round(dt_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),"%")

Training Accuracy : 100.0 %
Testing Accuracy : 75.41 %
In [ ]:

10.3 Random Forest¶

Random Forest combines multiple Decision Trees to improve prediction accuracy and reduce overfitting. It is evaluated to determine whether an ensemble model performs better than individual models.

In [46]:
rf_model=RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)

Train the Model¶

In [47]:
rf_model.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[47]:
RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)

Make Predictions¶

In [48]:
rf_pred=rf_model.predict(X_test)

Training and Testing Accuracy¶

In [49]:
print("Training Accuracy :", round(rf_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),"%")
print("Testing Accuracy :", round(rf_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),"%")

Training Accuracy : 100.0 %
Testing Accuracy : 86.89 %

11. Model Evaluation¶

11.1 Logistic Regression Evaluation¶

Confusion Matrix¶

In [50]:
cm_lr=confusion_matrix(y_test,lr_pred)
In [51]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
sns.heatmap(cm_lr,annot=True,fmt="d",cmap="Blues")
plt.title("Logistic Regression - Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("Actual Label")
plt.show()

Classification Report¶

In [52]:
print(classification_report(y_test,lr_pred))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.89      0.86      0.88        29
           1       0.88      0.91      0.89        32

    accuracy                           0.89        61
   macro avg       0.89      0.88      0.88        61
weighted avg       0.89      0.89      0.89        61

ROC Curve¶

In [53]:
lr_prob=lr_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]
fpr,tpr,thresholds=roc_curve(y_test,lr_prob)
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(fpr,tpr,label="Logistic Regression")
plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"--")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("Logistic Regression ROC Curve")
plt.legend()
plt.savefig("roc_curve.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

AUC Score¶

In [54]:
lr_auc=roc_auc_score(y_test,lr_prob)
print("AUC Score :",round(lr_auc,3))

AUC Score : 0.921

11.2 Decision Tree Evaluation¶

Confusion Matrix¶

In [55]:
cm_dt=confusion_matrix(y_test,dt_pred)
In [56]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
sns.heatmap(cm_dt,annot=True,fmt="d",cmap="Greens")
plt.title("Decision Tree - Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("Actual Label")
plt.show()

Classification Report¶

In [57]:
print(classification_report(y_test,dt_pred))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.73      0.76      0.75        29
           1       0.77      0.75      0.76        32

    accuracy                           0.75        61
   macro avg       0.75      0.75      0.75        61
weighted avg       0.75      0.75      0.75        61

ROC Curve¶

In [58]:
dt_prob=dt_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]
fpr_dt,tpr_dt,thresholds=roc_curve(y_test,dt_prob)
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(fpr_dt,tpr_dt,label="Decision Tree")
plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"--")
plt.title("Decision Tree ROC Curve")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

AUC Score¶

In [59]:
dt_auc=roc_auc_score(y_test,dt_prob)
print("AUC Score :",round(dt_auc,3))

AUC Score : 0.754

11.3 Random Forest Evaluation¶

Confusion Matrix¶

In [60]:
cm_rf=confusion_matrix(y_test,rf_pred)
In [61]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
sns.heatmap(cm_rf,annot=True,fmt="d",cmap="Oranges")
plt.title("Random Forest - Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("Actual Label")
plt.show()

Classification Report¶

In [62]:
print(classification_report(y_test,rf_pred))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.84      0.90      0.87        29
           1       0.90      0.84      0.87        32

    accuracy                           0.87        61
   macro avg       0.87      0.87      0.87        61
weighted avg       0.87      0.87      0.87        61

ROC Curve¶

In [63]:
rf_prob=rf_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]
fpr_rf,tpr_rf,thresholds=roc_curve(y_test,rf_prob)
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(fpr_rf,tpr_rf,label="Random Forest")
plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"--")
plt.title("Random Forest ROC Curve")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

AUC Score¶

In [64]:
rf_auc=roc_auc_score(y_test,rf_prob)
print("AUC Score :",round(rf_auc,3))

AUC Score : 0.932

12. Hyperparameter Tuning¶

GridSearchCV is used to identify the best combination of Decision Tree hyperparameters by performing cross-validation on multiple parameter values. This helps improve the model's generalization performance.

12.1 Define Parameter Grid¶

In [65]:
param_grid={
    "criterion": ["gini", "entropy"],
    "max_depth": [3,5,7,None],
    "min_samples_split": [2,5,10],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1,2,4]
}

12.2 GridSearchCV¶

In [66]:
grid_search= GridSearchCV(estimator=dt_model,param_grid=param_grid,cv=5,scoring="accuracy",n_jobs=-1)

12.3 Train GridSearchCV¶

In [67]:
grid_search.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[67]:
GridSearchCV(cv=5, estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42), n_jobs=-1,
             param_grid={'criterion': ['gini', 'entropy'],
                         'max_depth': [3, 5, 7, None],
                         'min_samples_leaf': [1, 2, 4],
                         'min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10]},
             scoring='accuracy')
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
GridSearchCV(cv=5, estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42), n_jobs=-1,
             param_grid={'criterion': ['gini', 'entropy'],
                         'max_depth': [3, 5, 7, None],
                         'min_samples_leaf': [1, 2, 4],
                         'min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10]},
             scoring='accuracy')
DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy', max_depth=5, min_samples_leaf=2,
                       min_samples_split=5, random_state=42)
DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy', max_depth=5, min_samples_leaf=2,
                       min_samples_split=5, random_state=42)

12.4 Best Parameters¶

In [68]:
print("Best Parameters:")
print(grid_search.best_params_)

Best Parameters:
{'criterion': 'entropy', 'max_depth': 5, 'min_samples_leaf': 2, 'min_samples_split': 5}

12.5 Best Cross Validation Score¶

In [69]:
print("Best Cross Validation Score:")
print(round(grid_search.best_score_,3))

Best Cross Validation Score:
0.773

12.6 Best Tuned Decision Tree¶

In [70]:
best_dt_model=grid_search.best_estimator_

12.7 Prediction Using Tuned Model¶

In [71]:
best_dt_pred=best_dt_model.predict(X_test)

12.8 Tuned Decision Tree Accuracy¶

In [72]:
print("Training Accuracy :",round(best_dt_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),"%")
print("Testing Accuracy :",round(best_dt_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),"%")

Training Accuracy : 91.74 %
Testing Accuracy : 75.41 %

13. Feature Importance¶

Decision Tree Feature Importance¶

In [73]:
feature_importance=pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature":X.columns,
    "Importance":best_dt_model.feature_importances_
})
In [74]:
feature_importance=feature_importance.sort_values(by="Importance",ascending=False)
In [75]:
feature_importance
Out[75]:
Feature Importance
2 cp 0.245316
11 ca 0.171277
0 age 0.157691
4 chol 0.145206
12 thal 0.111056
9 oldpeak 0.078008
1 sex 0.046912
7 thalach 0.025687
8 exang 0.018847
3 trestbps 0.000000
6 restecg 0.000000
5 fbs 0.000000
10 slope 0.000000
In [76]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.barplot(data=feature_importance,x="Importance",y="Feature")
plt.title("Decision Tree Feature Importance")
plt.savefig("feature_importance.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

14. Cross Validation¶

Cross Validation evaluates the model on multiple train-test splits to estimate how well it generalizes to unseen data. It provides a more reliable performance estimate than a single train-test split.

In [77]:
lr_cv=LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000,random_state=42)
cv_scores=cross_val_score(lr_cv,X,y,cv=5,scoring="accuracy")
In [78]:
print("Cross Validation Scores:",cv_scores)
print("Average Cross Validation Accuracy:",round(cv_scores.mean()*100,2),"%")

Cross Validation Scores: [0.83606557 0.86885246 0.78688525 0.81666667 0.8       ]
Average Cross Validation Accuracy: 82.17 %

15. Model Comparison¶

Accuracy Comparison¶

In [79]:
comparison=pd.DataFrame({
    "Model":[
        "Logistic Regression",
        "Decision Tree",
        "Random Forest"
    ],   
    "Training Accuracy":[
        round(lr_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),
        round(dt_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2),
        round(rf_model.score(X_train,y_train)*100,2)
    ],    
    "Testing Accuracy":[
        round(lr_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),
        round(dt_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2),
        round(rf_model.score(X_test,y_test)*100,2)
    ]
})
comparison
Out[79]:
Model Training Accuracy Testing Accuracy
0 Logistic Regression 84.3 88.52
1 Decision Tree 100.0 75.41
2 Random Forest 100.0 86.89

Accuracy Comparison Chart¶

In [80]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.barplot(data=comparison,x="Model",y="Testing Accuracy")
plt.title("Testing Accuracy Comparison")
plt.ylabel("Accuracy (%)")
plt.savefig("model_comparison.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

Best Model Selection¶

In [81]:
best_model=comparison.loc[comparison["Testing Accuracy"].idxmax()]
best_model
Out[81]:
Model                Logistic Regression
Training Accuracy                   84.3
Testing Accuracy                   88.52
Name: 0, dtype: object
In [82]:
print("Best Model :",best_model["Model"])
print("Testing Accuracy :",best_model["Testing Accuracy"],"%")

Best Model : Logistic Regression
Testing Accuracy : 88.52 %

16. Save and Load Best Model¶

Save Best Model¶

In [83]:
joblib.dump(lr_model,"heart_disease_model.pkl")
Out[83]:
['heart_disease_model.pkl']

Load Saved Model¶

In [84]:
loaded_model=joblib.load("heart_disease_model.pkl")

17. New Patient Prediction¶

New Patient Data¶

In [85]:
new_patient=[[55,1,2,140,250,0,1,150,0,1.5,2,0,2]]

Prediction¶

In [86]:
prediction=loaded_model.predict(new_patient)

Result¶

In [87]:
if prediction[0]==0:
    print("Prediction : No Heart Disease")
else:
    print("Prediction : Heart Disease")

Prediction : Heart Disease

18. Conclusion¶

This project developed a machine learning model to predict the presence of heart disease using patient clinical data.

Three classification algorithms were evaluated:

  • Logistic Regression
  • Decision Tree
  • Random Forest

Among these models, Logistic Regression achieved the best testing accuracy (88.52%) and demonstrated stable performance without overfitting. Decision Tree showed clear signs of overfitting, while Random Forest performed well but slightly lower than Logistic Regression on the testing data.

This project demonstrates a complete machine learning workflow including data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, feature scaling, model building, evaluation, hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation, feature importance analysis, and model comparison.

Future Improvements¶

  • Train the model on a larger dataset.
  • Perform advanced hyperparameter tuning.
  • Try additional machine learning algorithms such as XGBoost and Support Vector Machine.
  • Deploy the trained model as a web application using Streamlit or Flask.
In [ ]:
